text:
RESTAGING CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, currently on enzalutamide 160 mg daily and ongoing ADT (leuprolide acetate) with castrate testosterone levels. Restaging study at 6-month mark following enzalutamide initiation to assess disease status. Baseline PSMA PET/CT at time of CRPC declaration demonstrated a right posterior iliac crest bone lesion (SUVmax 3.8) and a right iliac chain lymph node (1.1 cm, SUVmax 4.2). Current PSA 0.7 ng/mL.

---

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous contrast (iohexol, 125 mL, weight-based dosing). Axial images acquired at 1.25 mm collimation with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats. Scout topogram reviewed. Lung windows, soft tissue windows, and bone windows reviewed systematically.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations identified. No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. Central airways are patent. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The thoracic aorta is unremarkable in caliber without aneurysmal dilatation. Cardiac silhouette is within normal limits for size. Small bilateral coronary artery calcifications noted, consistent with known cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, former smoking history). No pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is homogeneous in echotexture and normal in size. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on this examination. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.
Gallbladder/Biliary: Gallbladder is unremarkable without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct within normal limits.
Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.
Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and attenuation without ductal dilatation or focal mass lesion.
Adrenal glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are thin and unremarkable. No adrenal nodules or enlargement. (Of note, patient is on long-term ADT and abiraterone was previously administered; adrenal morphology appears atrophic but without focal lesion.)
Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size with maintained corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi. Bilateral small cortical cysts, the largest measuring approximately 0.8 cm in the right kidney -- stable-appearing, benign.
Bowel: No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. No free intraperitoneal air or fluid.
Retroperitoneum/Lymph nodes: No enlarged retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph nodes above the aortic bifurcation. Aorta is normal in caliber.

PELVIS:
Prostate/Seminal vesicles: The prostate gland demonstrates a heterogeneous, shrunken appearance with post-radiation fibrosis and post-hormonal therapy atrophy. The prostate bed is irregular but without a discrete new soft tissue mass. Seminal vesicles are atrophic and not separately distinguishable from surrounding post-radiation changes -- overall appearance consistent with treatment effect and hormonal suppression.
Bladder: The urinary bladder is normally distended without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defects. Post-radiation changes in the posterior bladder wall are similar to prior.
Rectum: No rectal mass or significant wall thickening. Mild mucosal irregularity in the anterior rectal wall consistent with known prior radiation changes (radiation proctitis).
Pelvic lymph nodes: There is persistent mild enlargement of a right external iliac chain lymph node measuring approximately 1.0 cm in short axis, unchanged from the 1.1 cm measurement on the PSMA PET/CT performed at the time of CRPC declaration. No new pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No left iliac chain lymphadenopathy.
Bone windows: The osseous structures of the pelvis and lower lumbar spine are examined in conjunction with the concurrent bone scan (below). No lytic or blastic lesion with definite cortical disruption is identified on CT windows. The right posterior iliac crest shows subtle sclerotic density in the area corresponding to the known PSMA-avid lesion, stable from prior imaging.

---

WHOLE-BODY TECHNETIUM-99m METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE (Tc-99m MDP) BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT OF THE PELVIS

TECHNIQUE:
Intravenous administration of Tc-99m MDP (25 mCi). Whole-body planar images acquired in anterior and posterior projections at 3 hours post-injection. Supplemental SPECT/CT of the pelvis performed for further characterization of the known right posterior iliac crest focus.

FINDINGS:
There is a single area of mildly increased radiotracer uptake in the right posterior iliac crest, similar in intensity and morphology to the appearance on the baseline scan obtained at the time of CRPC declaration (which was barely perceptible on bone scan at that time, with the lesion having been detected initially by PSMA PET/CT). On SPECT/CT correlation, this uptake corresponds to a small sclerotic focus within the right posterior iliac crest, without cortical disruption or soft tissue extension, consistent with a treated/sclerotic bone metastasis.

No new areas of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified throughout the axial or appendicular skeleton. In particular, there is no new abnormal uptake in the spine, ribs, pelvis (beyond the known right iliac crest focus), sternum, long bones, or skull.

The kidneys demonstrate symmetric tracer excretion without focal areas of abnormal uptake. Bladder activity is as expected.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. The known right posterior iliac crest bone lesion demonstrates stable appearance on both bone scan and CT bone windows, with no convincing interval change. The lesion appears sclerotic, which may reflect a response to ongoing systemic therapy.

2. No new bone lesions identified anywhere in the skeleton. The bone scan is otherwise unremarkable.

3. The right external iliac chain lymph node measures approximately 1.0 cm in short axis, essentially unchanged from prior (1.1 cm on prior PSMA PET/CT). No new pelvic, retroperitoneal, or thoracic lymphadenopathy.

4. No hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral metastases identified.

5. Post-treatment changes in the prostate bed and pelvis are consistent with prior definitive radiation and hormonal therapy. Atrophic prostate without discrete local recurrence on CT.

6. Incidental note of mild bilateral coronary artery calcification and small bilateral renal cortical cysts, both of benign significance.

Overall, the findings are consistent with stable metastatic disease without evidence of radiographic progression. Clinical correlation with current PSA trend is recommended.

Signed: Attending Radiologist, Genitourinary Radiology
